input_messages.content: {"suggestions": ["Deploy Snort IPS inline to detect and block network scanning patterns (SYN floods, port sweeps, OS fingerprinting)", "Implement network segmentation with firewall rules to restrict host scanning between different network segments", "Configure host-based firewalls on endpoints to block inbound connections from unauthorized internal sources"], "chosen_mitigation": "Deploy Snort IPS inline to detect and block network scanning patterns (SYN floods, port sweeps, OS fingerprinting)", "no_mitigation_left": false, "current_mitigations_counter": 1, "reached_mitigation_limit": false, "mitigation_start_timestamp": "2026-01-01T18:26:27.995643"}

Now I need to add a Snort device:

<function_calls>
<invoke name="add_network_device">
<parameter name="device_type" string="true">snort</parameter>
<parameter name="device_name" string="true">snort-ips-1</parameter>
</invoke>
</function_calls>

{"comment": "The implementer added a Snort IPS device as suggested. This is a correct first step as Snort is pre-configured to automatically detect and block malicious scanning traffic, which addresses the ongoing network scanning attack from the compromised Ubuntu host. However, simply adding the device is not sufficient; it must be placed inline in the network path where it can inspect traffic between the attacker and target networks. The Snort device needs to be linked into the topology, ideally between the compromised host's network (192.168.2.x) and the rest of the network to intercept scanning attempts towards 192.168.1.1. I will approve this addition but note that proper placement/linking is required for the next step.", "approved": true, "mitigation_currently_implemented": "Deploy Snort IPS inline to detect and block network scanning patterns (SYN floods, port sweeps, OS fingerprinting)", "mitigations_counter": 1, "mitigation_iteration": 1, "reached_mitigation_iteration_limit": false}
